Here is the pattern summary across all 31 findings:

## Severity Breakdown
- High: 7 findings (4 frustration/correction on E2E validator, 3 decisions)
- Medium: 18 findings
- Low: 6 findings

## Type Breakdown
- Corrections: 16 (user fixing agent behavior)
- Decisions: 6 (human judgment calls)
- Frustration: 5 (escalating tone or explicit complaints)
- Praise: 4 (positive signals)
- Redirections: 2 (user changing approach)

## Top Themes

### 1. E2E Validator Black-Box Enforcement (CRITICAL — 7 findings)
The dominant theme of this sprint. The E2E validator repeatedly fell back to file inspection instead of behavioral execution. Required 3+ rounds of fixes to the agent definition. Root causes: no cmux knowledge, optional language treated as opt-out, MANUAL used as escape hatch, orchestrator running inline instead of spawning subagent.

Key pattern: **Optional language in agent definitions is treated as permission to NOT do the thing.** "Can use cmux" becomes "doesn't use cmux." Only "MUST use cmux" works.

### 2. Workflow Fidelity Gaps (5 findings)
Agents bypassed workflow steps: running E2E inline instead of spawning, not parallelizing research agents, skipping subagent spawns. The workflow-fidelity rule exists but enforcement is inconsistent.

### 3. Story Completeness Gaps (4 findings)
Stories consistently missed: templates for new artifact types, draft markers on stubs, worktree cleanup in quality gates, proper epic assignment. User catches these during review every time.

### 4. cmux as Fundamental Infrastructure (4 findings)
User treats cmux as a core capability that all agents should have. Gap between this expectation and actual state led to creating a global rule. cmux surface type confusion also appeared (markdown viewer vs terminal pane).

### 5. Conversational Over Autonomous (3 findings)
User consistently prefers back-and-forth dialogue for planning/assessment decisions rather than batch-approval or autonomous one-shot patterns. Re-prioritization, assessment findings, and story scope discussions all follow this pattern.

### 6. Research Quality (3 findings)
Parallelism not leveraged, temporal currency not enforced, agent self-sufficiency with browser automation incomplete.

### 7. Positive Signals
- Create-story skill quality is strong (immediate approvals)
- Agent proactively suggesting features (intake skill) is valued
- The template catch-and-fix cycle works (flag → fix → approve)
- User thinks about downstream distribution, showing momentum-as-product mindset

### 8. Session Length / Context Loss (2 findings)
Very long sessions (the main sprint-dev session had multiple context compaction events) caused topics to be forgotten. User had to re-ask about previously discussed issues.

## Intervention Rate
Of the 267 messages, approximately 80-90 are substantive user messages (excluding system messages, task notifications, skill invocations, and simple "yes"/"approved" confirmations). Of those, roughly 18 are corrections and 5 are frustration signals — an intervention rate of approximately 25-28%. The E2E validator alone accounts for about 40% of all corrections.

Ready for any follow-up queries.
</teammate-message>

The Stop hook at ~/.claude/stop-hook-git-check.sh is blocking because your repo has no remote and there's an uncommitted change:

modified: .claude/momentum/gate-findings.txt

The hook fires on every turn end and requires all changes to be committed. Since there's no remote, the push check falls through gracefully — but the uncommitted file check catches it first.

Two options:

Option A — Just commit the modified file (simplest):

cd /home/user/repo
git add .claude/momentum/gate-findings.txt
git commit -m "chore: update gate findings"

With no remote, the hook will then exit cleanly.

Option B — Add a remote and push:

git remote add origin <your-repo-url>
git push -u origin main

Option A is faster if you're not ready to set up a remote yet. Once the uncommitted change is committed, the hook will stop blocking plan approval.

Once the remote is set, the signing server will have a source to reference and the commit (and subsequent push) should succeed.

Split candidates — Items bundling two distinct solvable problems with different epics or independent value. Split into separate items.

Conflicts — Items that contradict each other in scope or priority. Surface explicitly.

Profile: scan (discovery-only — find issues without running a fix loop; developer reviews findings before deciding what to repair)

Domain expert: planning / intake agent (content is story stubs, not code)

Lens focus (Tier 1 + Tier 2 dimensions most relevant here):

Lens    What to check
structural_validity    Every stub has valid YAML frontmatter (title, story_key, status: backlog, epic_slug), DRAFT markers on all required sections, stub-template pattern followed
correctness    epic_slug values exist in epics.md; priority values are valid (critical/high/medium/low); all new slugs present in stories/index.json
traceability    Every stub can be traced to a source document (triage doc or process backlog item ID) — either via inline note or obvious title match
completeness    All 38 triage items are accounted for (skip/merge/stub); all PT items with identified gaps have a stub or documented closure reason
logical_soundness    Consolidation decisions (merges, splits) have a stated rationale; epic assignments are consistent with epic scope in epics.md
consistency    No slug collisions in stories/index.json; no title/scope contradictions between merged items and their host stories
Adversary framing: The Adversary agent specifically challenges whether any item was silently dropped (completeness), whether any epic assignment is a mismatch to the epic's defined scope, and whether any "merge" enriches an existing story in a way that contradicts its current ACs or scope.

Enumerator framing: The Enumerator agent lists all potential issues against source material — checking stub content against the triage doc entries and process backlog descriptions for accuracy and fidelity.

Pass threshold: ≥95 (Clean). Any critical or high finding blocks proceeding — fix before considering the triage complete.

# BMad Help

## Purpose

Help the user understand where they are in their BMad workflow and what to do next, and also answer broader questions when asked that could be augmented with remote sources such as module documentation sources.

## Desired Outcomes

When this skill completes, the user should:

1. **Know where they are** — which module and phase they're in, what's already been completed
2. **Know what to do next** — the next recommended and/or required step, with clear reasoning
3. **Know how to invoke it** — skill name, menu code, action context, and any args that shortcut the conversation
4. **Get offered a quick start** — when a single skill is the clear next step, offer to run it for the user right now rather than just listing it
5. **Feel oriented, not overwhelmed** — surface only what's relevant to their current position; don't dump the entire catalog
6. **Get answers to general questions** — when the question doesn't map to a specific skill, use the module's registered documentation to give a grounded answer

## Data Sources

- **Catalog**: `{project-root}/_bmad/_config/bmad-help.csv` — assembled manifest of all installed module skills
- **Config**: `config.yaml` and `user-config.yaml` files in `{project-root}/_bmad/` and its subfolders — resolve `output-location` variables, provide `communication_language` and `project_knowledge`
- **Artifacts**: Files matching `outputs` patterns at resolved `output-location` paths reveal which steps are possibly completed; their content may also provide grounding context for recommendations
- **Project knowledge**: If `project_knowledge` resolves to an existing path, read it for grounding context. Never fabricate project-specific details.
- **Module docs**: Rows with `_meta` in the `skill` column carry a URL or path in `output-location` pointing to the module's documentation (e.g., llms.txt). Fetch and use these to answer general questions about that module.

## CSV Interpretation

The catalog uses this format:

```
module,skill,display-name,menu-code,description,action,args,phase,after,before,required,output-location,outputs
```

**Phases** determine the high-level flow:
- `anytime` — available regardless of workflow state
- Numbered phases (`1-analysis`, `2-planning`, etc.) flow in order; naming varies by module

**Dependencies** determine ordering within and across phases:
- `after` — skills that should ideally complete before this one
- `before` — skills that should run after this one
- Format: `skill-name` for single-action skills, `skill-name:action` for multi-action skills

**Required gates**:
- `required=true` items must complete before the user can meaningfully proceed to later phases
- A phase with no required items is entirely optional — recommend it but be clear about what's actually required next

**Completion detection**:
- Search resolved output paths for `outputs` patterns
- Fuzzy-match found files to catalog rows
- User may also state completion explicitly, or it may be evident from the current conversation

**Descriptions carry routing context** — some contain cycle info and alternate paths (e.g., "back to DS if fixes needed"). Read them as navigation hints, not just display text.

## Response Format

For each recommended item, present:
- `[menu-code]` **Display name** — e.g., "[CP] Create PRD"
- Skill name in backticks — e.g., `bmad-create-prd`
- For multi-action skills: action invocation context — e.g., "tech-writer lets create a mermaid diagram!"
- Description if present in CSV; otherwise your existing knowledge of the skill suffices
- Args if available

**Ordering**: Show optional items first, then the next required item. Make it clear which is which.

## Constraints

- Present all output in `{communication_language}`
- Recommend running each skill in a **fresh context window**
- Match the user's tone — conversational when they're casual, structured when they want specifics
- If the active module is ambiguous, retrieve all meta rows remote sources to find relevant info also to help answer their question

The Stop hook at ~/.claude/stop-hook-git-check.sh is blocking because your repo has no remote and there's an uncommitted change:

modified: .claude/momentum/gate-findings.txt

The hook fires on every turn end and requires all changes to be committed. Since there's no remote, the push check falls through gracefully — but the uncommitted file check catches it first.

Two options:

Option A — Just commit the modified file (simplest):

cd /home/user/repo
git add .claude/momentum/gate-findings.txt
git commit -m "chore: update gate findings"

With no remote, the hook will then exit cleanly.

Option B — Add a remote and push:

git remote add origin <your-repo-url>
git push -u origin main

Option A is faster if you're not ready to set up a remote yet. Once the uncommitted change is committed, the hook will stop blocking plan approval.

Merge candidates — Items addressing the same problem from different sources. Produce a single item with union of context.

Split candidates — Items bundling two distinct solvable problems with different epics or independent value. Split into separate items.

Conflicts — Items that contradict each other in scope or priority. Surface explicitly.

Lens focus (Tier 1 + Tier 2 dimensions most relevant here):

Lens What to check
structural_validity Every stub has valid YAML frontmatter (title, story_key, status: backlog, epic_slug), DRAFT markers on all required sections, stub-template pattern followed
correctness epic_slug values exist in epics.md; priority values are valid (critical/high/medium/low); all new slugs present in stories/index.json
traceability Every stub can be traced to a source document (triage doc or process backlog item ID) — either via inline note or obvious title match
completeness All 38 triage items are accounted for (skip/merge/stub); all PT items with identified gaps have a stub or documented closure reason
logical_soundness Consolidation decisions (merges, splits) have a stated rationale; epic assignments are consistent with epic scope in epics.md
consistency No slug collisions in stories/index.json; no title/scope contradictions between merged items and their host stories
Adversary framing: The Adversary agent specifically challenges whether any item was silently dropped (completeness), whether any epic assignment is a mismatch to the epic's defined scope, and whether any "merge" enriches an existing story in a way that contradicts its current ACs or scope.

Enumerator framing: The Enumerator agent lists all potential issues against source material — checking stub content against the triage doc entries and process backlog descriptions for accuracy and fidelity.

Pass threshold: ≥95 (Clean). Any critical or high finding blocks proceeding — fix before considering the triage complete.

ARGUMENTS: Decision: Build momentum:feature-status skill using Mermaid diagrams + status tables rendered in cmux markdown viewer. This introduces a Feature artifact (finite, user-observable unit with acceptance condition) as the missing layer between PRD and stories. Two rendering paths: product projects (Nornspun — flow/connection/quality feature tracking) and practice projects (Momentum — skill topology + SDLC coverage map). Based on research showing no mainstream tool addresses "distance to working software" at the feature level, and that AI-assisted development specifically widens the gap between stories done and features working. Build order: Feature schema → feature-status skill → Impetus greeting integration → sprint-boundary context compression.
